does: This paper investigates whether unlearning harmful content in the textual domain alone can effectively prevent safety violations across multiple modalities in Vision-Language Models. The authors conduct empirical evaluation across six datasets, demonstrating that textual unlearning substantially reduces attack success rates to below 8% (and as low as 2%) for both text-only and vision-text multimodal attacks while maintaining model utility. The key insight leverages the architectural design of VLMs where diverse input modalities ultimately converge into a shared language space, suggesting that safety alignment in text may suffice for cross-modal protection without requiring expensive multi-modal safety training datasets.
connects: This work builds on the growing concern that adding vision capabilities to LLMs creates vulnerabilities that circumvent existing safety alignment techniques like Supervised Fine-tuning and RLHF. It responds to the practical challenge that collecting multi-modal safety training data is costly and difficult, extending prior work on unlearning and safety training in single-modality models to the multi-modal setting. The paper connects to architectural insights about how VLMs process information, where visual and textual inputs merge into a unified language representation, bridging research on model interpretability with safety alignment and suggesting that the bottleneck for harmful behavior occurs in the textual domain regardless of input modality.
enables: This finding opens pathways for more efficient safety alignment of multi-modal systems by showing that practitioners may not need to collect expensive multi-modal training data or conduct costly multi-modal RLHF. Future work could explore whether this approach generalizes to other modalities beyond vision, such as audio or video inputs, and whether the language-space bottleneck holds across different architectural designs. The result also suggests promising directions for studying where safety violations originate in multi-modal systems and could inform the design of inherently safer multi-modal architectures that preserve the language space's role as a security checkpoint while expanding into new modalities.
